chunk_summary: This section discusses high-calorie solid feeding strategies and school logistics for children aged 2-10 years with chronic cardiorespiratory disease, emphasizing malnutrition indicators and growth standards.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of high-calorie solids in managing pediatric malnutrition in chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
Learn school feeding logistics tailored for children aged 2-10 years with specific nutritional needs.
Apply WHO child growth standards to assess weight and height in this age group.
Identify evidence-based malnutrition indicators from Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics/ASPEN guidelines.
key_facts: Children with chronic cardiorespiratory disease often require high-calorie diets to meet energy demands.
WHO child growth standards provide benchmarks for weight and height in children aged 2-10 years.
Malnutrition indicators include weight-for-age and height-for-age deviations, as per ASPEN guidelines.
School feeding programs should prioritize nutrient-dense meals to support growth and energy needs.
Preschoolers (2-5 years) require approximately 1,000-1,400 kcal/day, depending on activity level (IAP guidelines).
reference_quote: Indicators for pediatric malnutrition include weight-for-age and height-for-age deviations, as outlined by the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics/ASPEN guidelines.
document_key_topics: High-calorie feeding strategies for children with chronic diseases
School feeding logistics for ages 2-10 years
